frameworks.name: serial dependence
frameworks.one_line: attractive temporal integration stabilizing perception across noisy discontinuous input

frameworks.name: perceptual adaptation
frameworks.one_line: local repulsive aftereffect recalibrating stimulus representation automatically

frameworks.name: cortical feedback processing
frameworks.one_line: high-level modulatory feedback on early cortical activity gates serial dependence

frameworks.name: visual backward masking
frameworks.one_line: method used to suppress feedback signals onto early cortical representations

core_question: Does cortical feedback gate attractive serial dependence and reveal an underlying repulsive adaptation?
key_assumptions: serial dependence reflects an active temporal stabilization process
visual backward masking selectively suppresses high-level modulatory feedback
early cortical activity can host local recalibration without feedback
manipulations: presence vs absence of visual backward masking
stimulus duration manipulated down to 50 ms
three psychophysical experiments varying feedback conditions
key_findings: backward masking abolishes the attractive serial dependence effect
without modulatory feedback a robust repulsive aftereffect emerges after only 50 ms
two opposing cortical mechanisms operate at short timescales
interpretations: cortical feedback is critical for attractive serial dependence
an automatic local repulsive recalibration coexists with feedback-driven stabilization
